data.title: More than half of Americans have 'very little' confidence in ICE: Poll
data.description:
More than half of Americans have “very little” confidence in Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), according to a poll taken in the days before and after Alex Pretti was shot and killed by a federal officer in Minneapolis.

In The Economist/YouGov poll, 55 percent of respondents said they had “very little” confidence in ICE, while 16 percent said they have “some” confidence in the agency. Sixteen percent said they have “quite a lot” of confidence in ICE and 14 percent said they have “a great deal.”

The poll, conducted between Jan. 23 and 26, comes amid rising anger directed at the Trump administration over its immigration agenda and actions in Minneapolis, specifically.

Earlier this month, an ICE officer fatally shot Renee Good, a mother of three, as she attempted to drive away. On Saturday, a Border Patrol agent shot and killed Pretti, an intensive care nurse for veterans.

A growing chorus of Republicans have called for an independent investigation into Pretti’s killing and de-escalation in Minnesota. Leaders of Trump’s immigration agencies have also been called to testify in the GOP-controlled Senate.

House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) on Tuesday warned of leading impeachment proceedings targeting Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem, whose department houses ICE and Border Patrol, if President Trump does not remove her from her role.

“Donald Trump must fire Kristi Noem immediately,” Jeffries said on the social platform X. “Or Democrats will initiate impeachment proceedings against her in the House. We can do this the easy way or the hard way.”

The Economist/YouGov poll took place from Jan. 23 to 26, featuring 1,684 people with a 3.3 percentage point margin of error.

data.title: Trump on Bovino: 'A pretty out there kind of guy'
data.description:
President Trump on Tuesday referred to Border Patrol commander Greg Bovino as “a pretty out there kind of guy” when asked if his decision to have Bovino leave Minneapolis was a “pull back.” 

Trump said in some cases being “out there” was “good” and then suggested that may have not been the case in Minnesota in the aftermath of outrage erupting over the shooting death of U.S Citizen and ICU nurse Alex Pretti by Border Patrol agents.

“I don’t think it’s a pullback. It’s a little bit of a change. Everyone in this room that has a business, you know you make little changes,” Trump told Fox News’ Will Cain during a swing through Iowa. 

“You know, Bovino’s very good but he’s a pretty out there kind of a guy, and in some cases that’s good, maybe it wasn’t good here,” he continued. 

Bovino had tangled with both Democrats and Republicans on social media as videos circulated of Pretti’s shooting. Some lawmakers called for a full investigation into the matter as multiple videos from various angles showed Pretti being tackled and then shot in the back.

Bovino told CNN on Sunday that Pretti was attempting to “massacre” law enforcement, but video of the incident shows the ICU nurse filming agents and attempting to assist a woman they had pushed to the ground before he was surrounded, disarmed and shot.

Other Trump officials also took to social media to give their assessment of Pretti’s killing.

White House deputy chief of staff Stephen Miller referred to Pretti as a “would-be assassin” who “tried to murder federal law enforcement” in a post on X that was retweeted by Vice President JD Vance. 

On Monday, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt tried to distance Trump from such remarks, saying the president was looking for a full review of the incident.

When asked in Iowa about such remarks by his administration, Trump said he “hadn’t heard that.”

data.title: Trump approval among independent voters hits new low: Poll
data.description:
President Trump’s support from independent voters dropped to a new low across both terms in office, according to the latest Economist/YouGov poll.

The survey, conducted over the weekend, shows Trump’s net job approval among independents at negative 40 percentage points, with 27 percent approving and 67 percent disapproving.

That marks a significant drop from the previous week, when Trump’s net approval was negative 31 percentage points among independents, and from earlier this month, when it stood at negative 28 percentage points.

The pollsters cautioned that “it’s too early to say how much this drop reflects a long-term decline in approval among Independents and how much is weekly survey variation.”

Meanwhile, among Republicans, Trump’s net approval is positive 72 percentage points, with 85 percent approving and 13 percent disapproving; among Democrats, his net approval stands at negative 86 percentage points, with 6 percent approving and 92 percent disapproving.

The survey results come amid ongoing concerns about the economy and renewed scrutiny of the federal immigration crackdown in Minnesota, where two U.S. citizens have been fatally shot in recent weeks by federal officers during protests.

The most recent shooting, which has sparked concern from even some of Trump’s own base, took place on Saturday, the second day of the four-day poll.

The survey, conducted Jan. 23-26, included 1,684 citizens and had a margin of error of 3.3 percentage points.

data.title: Fourth wind farm blocked by Trump is allowed to resume construction
data.description:
A fourth offshore wind project halted by the Trump administration was temporarily allowed to resume construction on Tuesday, marking yet another legal setback for the White House on the issue.

Federal Judge Brian Murphy, a Biden appointee, issued a preliminary injunction blocking the administration’s stop work order against Vineyard Wind.

Vineyard Wind, which would provide power to Massachusetts, is one of five projects that the Trump administration tried to hold up in December.

Separate judges have now allowed four of those projects to resume, while the fifth is still awaiting court proceedings.

In December, the Trump administration said that it would suspend the authority of five previously approved offshore wind projects to build.

It said it was doing so because of the potential for radar interference known as “clutter” from wind turbines.

Critics of the administration have argued that the national security concern is simply a pretext for the administration to block an energy source it does not like, as President Trump has frequently criticized wind energy.

According to its website, when complete, Vineyard Wind would be able generate enough power for 400,000 homes and businesses.

The project already has 44 operational wind turbines and was working on an additional 18. The Trump pause applied to the construction work that was not yet complete.

data.title: Immigration officials to testify before Congress amid growing backlash over crackdown
data.description:
The top officials from three immigration government agencies have agreed to testify before the House and Senate Homeland Security committees. 

Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) acting Director Todd Lyons, Customs and Border Protection (CBP) Commissioner Rodney Scott and U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services Director Joseph Edlow will appear before the House panel on Feb. 10 and the Senate panel on Feb. 12.

Tuesday’s testimony agreement follows formal requests from House Committee Chairman Andrew Garbarino (R-N.Y.) and Senate Committee Chairman Rand Paul (R-Ky.). 

“I appreciate the Department of Homeland Security for working with us to make this hearing happen,” Garbarino said in a post on the social platform X on Tuesday. “We’ll continue our oversight work to ensure these agencies are carrying out their mission and keeping our communities and law enforcement safe.”

All three agencies are under the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), which has led a massive immigration crackdown effort, “Operation Metro Surge,” in Minnesota, among other cities across the U.S. DHS said the operation is the largest in agency history, with the department deploying over 2,000 federal agents to the state. 

The crackdown has been the source of intense friction between Minnesotans and federal authorities, particularly since two fatal shootings this month. In Minneapolis, an ICE agent shot and killed Renee Good, a mother of three, amid a protest. Additionally, federal agents shot a Venezuelan migrant in the leg while he attempted to evade detainment. 

After federal authorities fatally shot 37-year-old intensive care nurse and Minnesota resident Alex Pretti in Minneapolis over the weekend, lawmakers on both sides of the aisle have called for an investigation into DHS’ operations in the state. 

Sen. Bill Cassidy (R-La.) called the situation “incredibly disturbing” in a post on the social platform X on Saturday. 

“The credibility of ICE and DHS are at stake,” Cassidy said. “There must be a full joint federal and state investigation. We can trust the American people with the truth.”

Additionally, Senate Democrats have said they will not back a six-bill funding package unless the DHS funding bill is taken out. It includes $10 billion for ICE. If the funding package is not passed by the Friday deadline, lawmakers will face a government shutdown.

data.title: Judge dismisses DOJ bid to obtain Oregon voter rolls
data.description:
A federal judge in Oregon dismissed the Department of Justice’s (DOJ) lawsuit against the state for refusing to provide its unredacted voter rolls.

U.S. District Judge Mustafa Kasubhai, an appointee of former President Biden, is set to issue a written opinion in the coming days, according to The Associated Press.

“The court dismissed this case because the federal government never met the legal standard to get these records in the first place,” Oregon Attorney General Dan Rayfield (D) told the AP in an emailed statement. 

“Oregonians deserve to know that voting laws can’t be used as a backdoor to grab their personal information,” he added.

Kasubhai’s decision comes as the DOJ is awaiting rulings for their lawsuits against more than 10 other Democratic-led states for not complying with its request to produce documents that tabulate their statewide voter registration lists.

It follows U.S. District Judge David O. Carter’s January dismissal of a DOJ lawsuit against California.

“There cannot be unbridled consolidation of all elections power in the executive (branch) without action from Congress,” Carter said. “This is antithetical to the promise of fair and free elections.”

However, the Trump administration has argued the opposite.

“Accurate voter rolls are the cornerstone of fair and free elections, and too many states have fallen into a pattern of noncompliance with basic voter roll maintenance,” Attorney General Pam Bondi previously said in a statement on the matter. 

“The Department of Justice will continue filing proactive election integrity litigation until states comply with basic election safeguards,” she added. 

Multiple states have supplied federal officials with publicly disclosable information but refused to provide additional information, arguing it would violate the Civil Rights Act.

Earlier this month, the DOJ sued Virginia for not handing over state voter data.

data.title: Virginia judge blocks Democratic-favored map from going into effect ahead of midterms
data.description:
A Virginia judge delivered a blow to Democrats on Tuesday, ruling that the party would not be allowed to use their new favorable House map in the state in time for the November midterms.

Tazewell County Circuit Court Judge Jack Hurley Jr. wrote in his ruling that Democrats violated legislative rules in how they convened for their special session on redistricting in the fall and that the first passage of the constitutional amendment on redistricting did not take place before the state’s 2025 elections.

In order for a constitutional amendment to go before voters, it must be passed in the state legislature twice, with an election intervening in between the sessions.

“There is no rational conclusion except that the ELECTION began on the first day of voting (September 19, 2025) and ended on November 4, 2025,” Hurley wrote. “Therefore, the Court FINDS that following the October 31, 2025 vote and passage of [the redistricting amendment] HAS NOT BEEN an ensuing general election of the House of Delegates.”

As a result, Hurley argued that Democrats had not successfully passed the constitutional measure twice between an election, meaning it could not be put before voters before the 2026 elections.

Democrats signaled that they would immediately appeal the ruling.

“Republicans court-shopped for a ruling because litigation and misinformation are the only tools they have left,” Charles Dongo, the campaign manager behind the campaign to redistrict in Virginia, said in a statement.

“We’re prepared for what comes next, and Virginians deserve both the right to vote and the chance to level the playing field.” 

Virginia Democrats convened in October and once again earlier this month to pass a constitutional amendment to go before voters in a spring referendum that would allow lawmakers to temporarily redistrict the state by October 2030.

Virginia has a 6-5 Democratic edge in its congressional delegation, but Democrats are eyeing a more favorable 10-1 or 9-2 map for November.

Virginia offers Democrats one of their best opportunities to pick up a handful of seats. The spring election, which Democrats were looking to schedule April 21, would be taking place around the same time Florida Republicans convene for their special session to draft new congressional lines.

“Nothing that happened today will dissuade us from continuing to move forward and put this matter directly to the voters,” top Virginia Democrats in the state legislature said in a joint statement.

“Republicans who can’t win at the ballot box are abusing the legal process in an attempt to sow confusion and block Virginians from voting.”

The campaign pushing to redistricting in the state also signaled they expected a fight in court.

data.title: Scarborough: 'Orwellian' DHS 'lying nonstop' about fatal shootings
data.description:
Political commentator Joe Scarborough on Tuesday slammed the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) for “lying nonstop” about two fatal shootings of U.S. citizens involving federal officers in Minneapolis, calling it “Orwellian.”

“The repeated lies, and people use the word ‘Orwellian’ … they overuse the word,” the host of MS NOW’s “Morning Joe” told co-host Jonathan Lemire.

Merriam-Webster’s dictionary defines Orwellian as “relating to or suggestive of the dystopian reality depicted in the novel ‘1984,’” written by British author and journalist George Orwell.

“Here, Orwellian [fits], because time and time again, you would see video of Americans being beaten or, in two cases, gunned down in the streets and DHS, their spokespeople and especially [Homeland Security Secretary] Kristi Noem, going back to Renee Good, lying through her teeth with her cowboy hat on, talking about snowbanks, just making s— up about dead Americans gunned down in the street,” Scarborough continued.

Scarborough said Noem and her department were “lying through their teeth” over the death of Good, who was shot and killed by an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer earlier this month.

Noem claimed that Good attempted an act of “domestic terrorism” by using her SUV to allegedly run the officer over, prompting the shooting. Video taken of the incident has caused outrage and debate, with some saying she tried to move her car out of the officer’s way.

“The first, remember, Renee Good saying, ‘I’m not mad,'” Scarborough said, referring to Good’s last known words recorded on video. “The last words spoken on earth to the man that shot her twice at point blank range, putting a bullet through her head. ‘I’m not mad.’ And then she turns and tries to leave. They lie about her and immediately Kristi Noem, without knowing any of the facts, lies through her teeth.”

Scarborough said the DHS response was the same with Alex Pretti, who was killed on Saturday after an altercation involving U.S. Border Patrol agents. Noem also accused Pretti of “domestic terrorism.” His shooting was caught from multiple angles by video, with some showing an officer stepping away with a gun taken off Pretti before he was shot in the back.

“I know so much played in to just the torrent of Republicans finally having the courage to stand up and speak out and all of this happening, but it seems to me a lot of people kept going back to all the lies that Kristi Noem has been telling over and over and over again,” Scarborough added.

Trump administration officials were previously in line with Noem’s claims about Good, but that shifted after Pretti’s death. White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt told reporters on Monday that President Trump “wants to let the facts and the investigation lead itself.”

“Again, this incident remains under investigation, and nobody here at the White House, including the president of the United States, wants to see Americans hurt or killed and losing their lives in American streets,” she said.

Some Republican lawmakers, facing a possible government shutdown if Senate Democrats block a bill to fund DHS after Saturday’s shooting, have also called for an impartial investigation into Pretti’s death.

On Sunday, Sen. Lisa Murkowski (R-Alaska) said the shooting raises “serious questions within the administration about the adequacy of immigration-enforcement training and the instructions officers are given on carrying out their mission.”

“The events in Minneapolis are incredibly disturbing,” Sen. Bill Cassidy (R-La.) said in a post on the social platform X. “The credibility of ICE and DHS are at stake. There must be a full joint federal and state investigation. We can trust the American people with the truth.”

data.title: Sam Altman says 'what's happening with ICE is going too far': Reports
data.description:
OpenAI CEO Sam Altman criticized Immigration Customs and Enforcement (ICE) for “going too far” following the killing of 37-year-old Alex Pretti, according to media reports.

“I love the US and its values of democracy and freedom and will be supportive of the country however I can; OpenAI will too,” Altman wrote in a message to employees obtained by The New York Times and Business Insider.

“But part of loving the country is the American duty to push back against overreach,” he continued. “What’s happening with ICE is going too far. There is a big difference between deporting violent criminals and what’s happening now, and we need to get the distinction right.”

Pretti was shot and killed by a Border Patrol agent Saturday in Minneapolis, where tensions were already running high following the fatal shooting of 37-year-old Renee Good earlier this month.

Altman, who has maintained a friendly relationship with President Trump in his second term, offered praise for Trump, calling him a “very strong leader” and saying he hopes the president “will rise to this moment and unite the country.”

“I am encouraged by the last few hours of response and hope to see trust rebuilt with transparent investigations,” he added.

Trump has reportedly pulled Greg Bovino, commander of the U.S. Border Patrol, out of Minneapolis and is sending in border czar Tom Homan instead, which is widely viewed as an effort to recalibrate the administration’s approach.

“As a company, we aim to stick to our convictions and not get blown around by changing fashions too much,” Altman said. “We didn’t become super woke when that was popular, we didn’t start talking about masculine corporate energy when that was popular, and we are not going to make a lot of performative statements now about safety or politics or anything else.”

Altman is the most high-profile tech figure to criticize ICE so far. In releasing an unrelated essay Monday, Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei suggested that “its emphasis on the importance of preserving democratic values and rights at home is particularly relevant” in the context of the “horror” in Minnesota.

Khosla Ventures founder Vinod Khosla also slammed ICE on Sunday, referring to its agents as “vigilantes” who are “running amuck empowered by a conscious-less administration.”

His comments came after Keith Rabois, managing director at Khosla Ventures, defended ICE in the wake of Saturday’s shooting, arguing that “no law enforcement has shot an innocent person.”

The tech industry, which had a fraught relationship with Trump in his first term, has maintained a largely positive rapport with the president in his second term. Tech leaders, including Altman, attended Trump’s inauguration last year and have appeared at the White House several times since.

data.title: Celebrities call on Americans to speak out against fatal shooting in Minneapolis
data.description:
Celebrities, including singers Katy Perry and Billie Eilish as well as “The Last of Us” star Pedro Pascal, are using social media to speak out against the recent killings of two U.S. citizens in Minneapolis by federal immigration enforcement officials.

On Saturday, Alex Pretti, a 37-year-old intensive care nurse, was shot and killed by a Border Patrol agent while 37-year-old Renee Good, a mother of three, was shot and killed by an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer earlier this month. Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem claim that Pretti was a “domestic terrorist” has sparked strong pushback on both sides of the aisle.

Perry, on Monday, asked her Instagram followers to write to their U.S. senators and urge them to oppose ICE funding. Pascal posted drawings of Pretti and Good on Sunday night, writing “Pretti Good reason for a nation strike,” while Eilish called Pretti “a real American hero” on Instagram, Reuters reported.

Eilish also pointed to other celebrities, urging them to speak up. Her post said, “hey my fellow celebrities u gonna speak up? Or” 

“Truth is a line of demarcation between a democratic government and authoritarian regime. Mr Pretti and Renee Good are dead. The American people deserve to know what happened,” Pascal wrote on Instagram, noting in a different post that, “if Congress does nothing, this becomes normal.”

On Instagram, actress and producer Jamie Lee Curtis shared photos of armed federal agents, accompanied by a caption “THIS IS AMERICA!” and a post of someone holding a waving U.S. flag, accompanied by the caption “I BELIEVE IN US!”

Mark Ruffalo, meanwhile, posted on Bluesky that Pretti’s shooting was a “cold blooded murder in the streets of the USA by an occupying military gang, creating havoc. We have fought wars in other countries for less than this.”

Several other public figures, including TV personality Martha Stewart, former NBA player and analyst Charles Barkley and actress Natalie Portman, have also spoked out against Pretti’s killing.

“I am disheartened and sad each and every day that we cannot demonstrate our sympathy for the beleaguered, that we are told immigrants, which most of us are or descended from are unwelcome, that we cannot show our frustration in peaceful demonstrations and that we can be attacked and even killed by Federal troops,” Stewart wrote on social media Monday.
